key_insight: The paper simulates contact-induced facial wrinkling by combining viscoelastic skin mechanics, high-order solid-shell FEM, and anatomically inspired skin ligaments to capture realistic wrinkle formation under transient contact.
engineering_relevance: It provides a physics-based tool for more realistic facial animation, digital humans, and biomechanical analysis of how tissue attachments and material response shape surface wrinkles.
method: Models skin as a viscoelastic layered material with time-dependent relaxation.
Uses high-order prismatic solid-shell finite elements to resolve through-thickness stresses and fine deformation modes.
Introduces continuum-based skin ligaments to represent heterogeneous facial attachments and mobility constraints.
evidence_signal: The method is evaluated on synthetic examples and compared against real-world footage, showing temporally coherent and visually realistic wrinkle patterns during transient contact.
watch_item: High-order FEM with layered viscoelastic contact and ligament constraints may be computationally demanding and sensitive to parameter calibration from anatomy or footage.
paper_title: Physics-Based Simulation of Contact-Induced Facial Wrinkling
